monthly_review.summary: You've significantly increased training intensity this month while maintaining volume, with notable gains in pace and aerobic training effect — though cadence and efficiency show early signs of fatigue adaptation.
monthly_review.highlight: Your average pace improved by 64 sec/km (from 10:12 to 9:08 min/km) despite a 6 bpm increase in average heart rate — indicating meaningful neuromuscular and metabolic efficiency gains.

goal_tracker.message: You need 21.6 more km across up to 8 remaining days — achievable with three 7–8 km runs (e.g., 7.2 km × 3 = 21.6 km), ideally spaced with at least one recovery run under 5 km.

insights.title: Efficiency gain confirms pacing precision
insights.body: Aerobic efficiency rose from 12.54 to 12.99 m/sec per bpm — a +3.6% improvement — aligning with your faster avg pace (548 vs 612 sec/km) and higher avg HR (141 vs 135 bpm), suggesting better oxygen utilization at submaximal intensities.

insights.title: Zone 3 dominance signals growing threshold capacity
insights.body: Zone 3 time increased from 36% to 35% (stable), but Zone 4 jumped from 11% to 12% and Zone 5 from 1% to 2%, while Z1 dropped sharply from 8% to 3% — indicating a strategic shift toward higher-intensity work, likely driving the pace improvement.

insights.title: Cadence surge with stride length variability suggests form adaptation
insights.body: Cadence rose +21 spm (128 → 149), yet stride length varied widely across recent runs (67.5–79.8 cm) and ground contact time increased on slower runs (342 ms on 10:08/km vs ~315–319 ms on 8:50–9:08/km runs), hinting at fatigue-related mechanics shifts during longer or easier efforts.

insights.title: Missing Z1 work may compromise autonomic recovery
insights.body: Z1 time fell from 8% to 3% — just 0.9 minutes per run on average — well below the 15–20% recommended for recreational runners to support parasympathetic restoration; this coincides with elevated avg HR (+6 bpm) and reduced run frequency.

recommendations.action: Do a 5 km Zone 1 run (HR ≤125 bpm) within next 48 hours, using your March 19 run (HR 123 bpm, 10:08/km) as pacing reference.
recommendations.reason: Z1 time is critically low (3%) and needed to restore autonomic balance and support upcoming higher-intensity sessions.

recommendations.action: Perform a 4 km tempo run at Zone 3 HR (148–155 bpm) with cadence ≥150 spm, mimicking your March 4 run (HR 154, cadence 150, pace 8:16/km).
recommendations.reason: Your Zone 3 work is strong and consistent — reinforcing this stimulus will solidify lactate threshold gains reflected in your improved pace and TE.

recommendations.action: Add 2 × 30-sec strides post-run after your next two easy runs, focusing on landing midfoot and maintaining 152+ spm (like your March 7 & 14 runs).
recommendations.reason: Cadence increased meaningfully (+21 spm), but stride length inconsistency suggests neuromuscular coordination can be further refined with targeted drills.

weekly_focus.description: Balance your hard-earned gains in pace and threshold capacity with deliberate low-HR recovery to sustain progress and prevent fatigue accumulation — especially given declining run frequency and minimal Z1 time.
suggested_sessions: 4 km Zone 3 tempo (HR 148–155 bpm, cadence ≥150 spm)
